name: Sticky Garlic Gochujang Chicken
why: Korean-inspired crispy chicken with a sweet-spicy gochujang glaze. The velveting technique (baking soda + cornstarch) gives the chicken an impossibly tender, restaurant-quality texture — a new technique to try that's surprisingly simple.
author: Henry
ingredients: 1¼ lb boneless, skinless chicken breasts (thighs or tenders work too), cut into ¾-inch pieces
3 tbsp avocado oil (or any high-heat oil)
2 tbsp gochujang paste (Korean chili paste, or more to taste)

steps: Cut chicken into small ¾-inch pieces.
In a bowl, combine 1 tbsp water, baking soda, kosher salt, sugar, white pepper, sesame oil, and 1 tbsp soy sauce. Stir until baking soda dissolves.
Add the chicken pieces, stir to coat, and let sit for 10 minutes. This velveting step tenderizes the meat.
Add cornstarch to the bowl with the chicken and stir to coat all pieces evenly.
In a small bowl, whisk together the remaining 2 tbsp soy sauce, honey, gochujang paste, and rice vinegar. Set aside.
Heat 2 tbsp oil in a deep skillet over medium-high heat.
Add half the chicken in a single layer and cook undisturbed for 2-3 minutes until golden on one side.
Flip and cook another 2-4 minutes until golden and cooked through. Remove to a plate.
Add more oil as needed and repeat with the remaining chicken.
Reduce skillet heat to medium-low and let the pan cool for 2 minutes.
Add a small drizzle of oil and sauté the minced garlic until fragrant, about 30 seconds.
Increase heat to high. Pour in the gochujang sauce and let it sizzle and bubble for 30 seconds.
Add the fried chicken back to the pan and toss vigorously until every piece is coated in the sticky glaze. Remove from heat.
Top with sliced green onions and toasted sesame seeds.
Serve immediately over fluffy jasmine rice with steamed vegetables on the side.

pro_tips: The velveting technique (baking soda + cornstarch) is a Chinese cooking secret that breaks down proteins for impossibly tender meat. Don't skip it — it makes a dramatic difference.
Gochujang varies in heat between brands. Start with 2 tbsp and taste the sauce before adding more. If you prefer milder heat, add a splash of extra honey.
